summary: Dan Shapiro explains that stock vesting is fundamentally a tax hack layered on top of another tax hack, but one that founders desperately need. Using a hypothetical where a departing co-founder walks away with equal equity despite contributing nothing, he illustrates why vesting protects committed founders. He argues that founder vesting is actually founder-friendly—the opposite of common perception—and should be paired with acceleration clauses to prevent losing value during acquisitions. The post provides detailed practical advice on vesting schedules, cliff periods, acceleration triggers, and 83(b) elections.
key_points: Stock grants are a crude hack to avoid taxes, and vesting is a hack on top of that hack—but a necessary one to align equity with actual contribution over time
Founder vesting is founder-friendly, not investor-friendly, because it protects committed founders from co-founders who leave early with unearned equity
Acceleration clauses (single-trigger and double-trigger) are essential complements to vesting that prevent founders from losing value during acquisitions
Founders should implement vesting before raising money to signal competence and reduce negotiation complexity
Filing 83(b) elections immediately upon incorporation is critical—missing the 30-day window is one of the most common and expensive founder mistakes
money_quotes: Stock grants are, at their heart, a crude hack to avoid taxes. Vesting is a hack to the hack – and one almost every founder needs.
It would be like if you paid someone four years worth of salary in a lump sum on their hire date.
He gets a call one afternoon that, for sitting on his duff for the past five years, he's worth a cool $40mm, same as Jill.
Founder vesting is founder friendly, the exact opposite of what most people think. You want it. Don't fight it.
Put founder vesting in place before you start to raise money. Investors will be impressed that you know what you're doing.
Stock is best used for people who are totally committed, so the stock accumulation shouldn't kick in until the commitment does.
If you're not sure if this applies to you, ask your lawyer. If they're not sure, fire them and hire someone else.
key_insight: Founder vesting, despite its reputation as an investor demand, is actually a founder-protective mechanism that should be adopted proactively at incorporation and paired with acceleration clauses.
